title: Tough Bacteria Survive Space Trip from Mars
summary: Scientists studied a special bacteria called Deinococcus radiodurans to see if it could survive being blasted off Mars by a big asteroid hit. They used machines to squish the bacteria with very high pressure, like what happens during an impact. The bacteria are known to be tough and can handle radiation and drying out. The tests showed that even under high pressure, many of the bacteria survived because their cell walls are strong. They also fixed any damage after the pressure. This means tiny life forms might travel between planets on rocks after asteroid hits, spreading life in space. The study helps us understand how life could move around in our solar system.

background_read: This article is about a science experiment. Scientists are curious if tiny living things, called bacteria, can survive big events like asteroid hits on Mars. They used a special bacteria that is very tough and tested it with machines that create high pressure. The results help us learn how life might spread in space, which is important for understanding if there is life on other planets. It's like a puzzle about how life moves around in our solar system.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists like Lily Zhao and K. T. Ramesh studied the bacteria.
WHAT: They tested if bacteria can survive high pressure from asteroid impacts on Mars.
WHERE: The study was done in a lab, but it relates to Mars and space.
WHY: To see if life could travel between planets after asteroid hits.

perspectives.description: Shows that bacteria can survive extreme conditions, suggesting life might spread in space.
